title: SI units
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Master SI units for Cambridge 9702: base units, derived units, prefixes and homogeneity checks — everything you need for P1 and P2.
learningObjectives: Recall and use the seven SI base units and express any derived unit in terms of them
Use standard prefixes (pico to tera) to convert between multiples and sub-multiples confidently
Check the homogeneity of physical equations by comparing units on both sides

simpleExplanation.title: SI Units in a Nutshell
simpleExplanation.summary: Every physical quantity in physics is built from just seven base units — things like the kilogram and the metre. All other units (speed, force, energy) are combinations of these, called derived units. Mastering this system lets you check equations and convert quantities without error.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of the seven base units like the letters of the alphabet. Just as every word in English is built from those 26 letters, every unit in physics is built from those seven base units — you just combine them in different ways.
steps: Memorise the 7 SI base quantities and their units: mass (kg), length (m), time (s), electric current (A), temperature (K), amount of substance (mol), luminous intensity (cd).
Practise deriving units for common quantities — write out the defining equation, substitute base units, and simplify.
Learn the prefix multipliers from pico (10⁻¹²) to tera (10¹²) and practise converting, e.g. 3.2 nm = 3.2 × 10⁻⁹ m.
For homogeneity questions, replace every symbol in an equation with its base units and check that both sides match exactly.

sections.content: Whether you are tackling a multiple-choice question on Paper 1 or a 'show that' derivation on Paper 2, **SI units** underpin almost every topic in Cambridge A Level Physics 9702. Get them right and you earn easy marks; get them wrong and errors cascade through your entire calculation. This lesson covers the **seven base units**, how to build **derived units** from scratch, the **prefix system**, and the powerful technique of **checking homogeneity** — exactly what Cambridge examiners test.

The International System of Units (SI) defines seven independent base quantities. Every other unit in physics is derived from these seven. You must know all of them for 9702:

• **Mass** — kilogram (kg)
• **Length** — metre (m)
• **Time** — second (s)
• **Electric current** — ampere (A)
• **Thermodynamic temperature** — kelvin (K)
• **Amount of substance** — mole (mol)
• **Luminous intensity** — candela (cd)

Note: the candela rarely appears in 9702 calculations, but Cambridge can still ask you to list it. Do not confuse **kelvin** (thermodynamic temperature) with degrees Celsius — kelvin is the SI base unit.
Derived Units — Building from Base Units

A **derived unit** is formed by multiplying or dividing base units according to the defining equation of the quantity. The method is always the same: write the defining equation, substitute SI base units for every quantity, then simplify.

**Example — the newton (N):**
Force = mass × acceleration
F = m × (Δv/Δt) = m × (m s⁻¹ / s)
So 1 N = 1 kg m s⁻²

**Example — the pascal (Pa):**
Pressure = force / area
P = N / m² = kg m s⁻² / m² = kg m⁻¹ s⁻²

**Example — the joule (J):**
Work = force × distance
W = N × m = kg m s⁻² × m = kg m² s⁻²

Always work systematically — start with the defining equation and never guess.

Derived unit = (base units of quantity A)^p × (base units of quantity B)^q × …
Example: [energy] = kg m² s⁻²

Cambridge 9702 regularly tests your ability to handle prefixes in calculations and unit conversions. The prefixes you must know are:

| Prefix | Symbol | Multiplier |
|--------|--------|------------|
| tera   | T      | 10¹²       |
| giga   | G      | 10⁹        |
| mega   | M      | 10⁶        |
| kilo   | k      | 10³        |
| centi  | c      | 10⁻²       |
| milli  | m      | 10⁻³       |
| micro  | μ      | 10⁻⁶       |
| nano   | n      | 10⁻⁹       |
| pico   | p      | 10⁻¹²      |

**Conversion tip:** When squaring or cubing a prefixed unit, apply the multiplier to the power as well. For example, 1 cm² = (10⁻² m)² = 10⁻⁴ m² — a very common source of errors in exam scripts.

An equation is **homogeneous** (dimensionally consistent) if the units on the left-hand side are identical to the units on the right-hand side. This is a powerful checking tool — and Cambridge examiners set dedicated marks for it.

**The method:**
1. Write the equation.
2. Replace each physical quantity with its SI base units.
3. Simplify both sides independently.
4. State whether the units are the same, and therefore whether the equation is or is not homogeneous.

**Important limitation:** Homogeneity can confirm that an equation is *possibly* correct, but it cannot prove it is correct. A dimensionally consistent equation could still have the wrong numerical constant (e.g. a missing factor of 2 or π). Always mention this in exam answers when asked.

items: There are exactly **7 SI base units** — memorise all seven including the candela, even though it rarely appears in calculations.
To find a derived unit, always **start from the defining equation** and substitute base units — never try to remember derived units in isolation.
When converting prefixed units that are **squared or cubed**, remember to apply the power to the prefix multiplier too (e.g. 1 mm³ = 10⁻⁹ m³, not 10⁻³ m³).
To check homogeneity, **replace every symbol** in the equation with its base units, simplify each side separately, and compare.
Homogeneity is a **necessary but not sufficient** condition for an equation to be correct — a missing dimensionless constant would not be detected.
Cambridge mark schemes award a specific mark for **stating the units of an answer** — always include them, even when the question does not explicitly ask.

sections.question: The period T of a simple pendulum is given by the equation T = 2π√(L/g), where L is the length of the pendulum and g is the gravitational field strength. Show that this equation is homogeneous with respect to its units.
sections.solution:
Step 1 — Identify the units of each quantity:
• T is a time: units = s
• L is a length: units = m
• g is gravitational field strength (acceleration): units = m s⁻²
• 2π is a dimensionless constant: no units

Step 2 — Write the right-hand side in base units:
√(L/g) = √(m / m s⁻²)
= √(m × s² × m⁻¹)
= √(s²)
= s

Step 3 — Compare both sides:
Left-hand side: s
Right-hand side: s

Step 4 — Conclusion:
Both sides have units of seconds, so the equation IS homogeneous. ✓

Note: The factor 2π is dimensionless and does not affect homogeneity.

sections.content: On Paper 2, when asked to 'show that an equation is homogeneous', Cambridge examiners expect you to show every step of working — substituting base units, simplifying, and writing a concluding statement. Simply writing 'both sides equal seconds' without showing the substitution will not earn full marks. Also, if the question then asks whether homogeneity proves the equation is correct, always say no — a dimensionless constant could be wrong.

sections.question: Express the unit of electrical resistance, the ohm (Ω), in terms of SI base units.
sections.solution:
Step 1 — Recall the defining equation for resistance:
V = IR, so R = V / I

Step 2 — Express voltage V in base units:
Voltage = energy / charge = J / C
J = kg m² s⁻²
C = A s
So V = kg m² s⁻² / (A s) = kg m² s⁻³ A⁻¹

Step 3 — Divide by current I (units: A):
R = V / I = kg m² s⁻³ A⁻¹ / A = kg m² s⁻³ A⁻²

Answer: 1 Ω = kg m² s⁻³ A⁻²

sections.content: A quick way to remember that resistance involves A⁻²: Ohm's law gives R = V/I, and since V itself contains A⁻¹ (from the energy-per-charge route), dividing by another A brings the total to A⁻². Build derived units from first principles every time rather than trying to memorise them — it's faster and more reliable under exam pressure.

faq.q: What SI base units do I need to know for Cambridge 9702 Physics?
faq.a: You need to know all seven SI base units: kilogram (kg) for mass, metre (m) for length, second (s) for time, ampere (A) for electric current, kelvin (K) for thermodynamic temperature, mole (mol) for amount of substance, and candela (cd) for luminous intensity. Our free 9702 course covers every topic tested in P1 and P2, with past-paper practice included.

faq.q: How do I check if an equation is homogeneous in Cambridge A Level Physics?
faq.a: To check homogeneity, replace every physical symbol in the equation with its SI base units, simplify both sides independently, and check whether they match. If they do, the equation is dimensionally consistent (homogeneous). Remember to state in your answer that homogeneity does not prove the equation is correct — a wrong dimensionless constant would not be detected. Our 9702 lesson and past-paper questions walk you through this technique step by step.

faq.q: Why do students lose marks on SI unit questions in Cambridge 9702 exams?
faq.a: The three most common mistakes are: forgetting to square or cube prefix multipliers when converting units (e.g. thinking 1 cm² = 10⁻² m² instead of 10⁻⁴ m²), not showing full working when deriving a unit, and omitting the concluding statement in homogeneity proofs. Our premium lesson highlights all of these examiner traps, and you can practise with real past-paper mark schemes directly on our platform.
